transcript:
: In my experience, the way you move your body throughout the day matters far more than any hour you might spend in the gym. Now, when we talk about longevity, we often get distracted by complicated diets, expensive supplements, or the latest biohacking trend. But if you look at the populations around the world who consistently live the longest, and if you look at what the emergency department data tells us about why people lose their independence as they age, it almost always comes down to movement. Not marathon running. Not CrossFit. Just simple, consistent, daily movements that maintain your muscle mass, your balance, your metabolic health, and your nervous system function.

So, what I'm going to do in this video today is walk you through the eight specific movements that I do every single day. These aren't exercises in the traditional sense. You won't need any equipment for most of them. You won't need a gym membership. But together, they address every major system in your body that declines with age. I'll explain the biology behind why each one matters, I'll show you what the research says about how they extend your healthspan, and then at the end, I'll give you the exact framework for how I fit all eight into my daily routine hour by hour.

If you're new to the channel, then hello. I'm Dr. Alex. I'm an emergency medicine doctor with a particular obsession for the things that actually make us live longer and what truly prevents the diseases that we see every day in the emergency department. Now, I've been refining this specific movement protocol over the last year or so, testing different combinations, different timings, and what I'm going to share with you today is what I've found works best for me and what aligns with the strongest evidence we have for longevity.

And what you'll notice here is that there's not a single supplement in sight. There is no piece of expensive equipment that you need to buy. Every single one of these movements is free, accessible, and evidence-based. Right then, let's get into it.

Movement number one is what I call the Circadian Walk, and this happens within the first thirty minutes of waking up. Now, this one seems almost too simple, but stay with me because the biology here is absolutely crucial. The first thing I do when I get out of bed, before I shower, before I eat, before anything, is that I get outside and I walk. It doesn't matter if it's raining, if it's windy, if it's freezing cold, which it very often is here in Yorkshire where we live. I get fresh air on my face and, critically, light into my eyes.

Your brain has this region called the suprachiasmatic nucleus that acts as your body clock's headquarters, and it's directly connected to your eyes through the retinal-hypothalamic tract. When bright natural light hits your eyes first thing in the morning, it sends an unambiguous signal that the day has started. This triggers a cortisol pulse, which is your alertness hormone, it increases serotonin production, and critically, it starts a countdown timer so that fourteen to sixteen hours later, your melatonin rises naturally and you feel sleepy at nighttime.

Even on a gray, overcast day, the light outside is many times brighter than indoor lighting. We're talking about 10,000 lux on a cloudy day versus maybe 500 lux indoors. And if you wake up before the sun rises, or you live somewhere where morning light is scarce in winter, then a bright light therapy lamp in the 10,000 lux range used for twenty to thirty minutes is a very good substitute. There was a study out of the University of Colorado which found that just one week of irregular light exposure was enough to shift the timing of your circadian clock by up to two hours, which downstream affects your sleep quality, your mood, and your metabolic markers.

I walk for about five to ten minutes. Just around the block. Sometimes with Steph, sometimes just with Mango, our golden Labrador, who is always desperate to get out anyway. And the movement itself matters here too. You're not just standing outside stationary. The gentle walking motion increases blood flow, it wakes up your joints, and it starts the process of lymphatic drainage that has been stagnant overnight. This isn't exercise. It's signaling.

Movement number two is the Thermal Transition, and this happens in the shower. Now, this isn't about buying an ice bath or a plunge pool. This is very simple. I have a normal warm shower, but at the end, I turn the temperature all the way to cold and I stand under it for as long as I can. When I first started doing this, I could only manage about ten seconds. It is genuinely uncomfortable if you're not used to it. But now, I can comfortably do this for two to three minutes. You adapt to it remarkably quickly.

What's happening here is that the cold water activates your sympathetic nervous system, your fight or flight response, which releases adrenaline and noradrenaline into your bloodstream. Your heart rate rises, your blood flow redirects to your muscles and your brain, and you become suddenly, intensely awake. But there's also evidence that regular cold exposure can raise your baseline noradrenaline over time, which is associated with better mood, sharper focus, and improved stress resilience.

The way it feels afterwards is difficult to describe. You step out and there's this surge of alertness like somebody has pressed a restart button on your brain. Your face feels tingly and that clarity lasts for hours. The best comparison I can offer is that adrenaline feeling you get after finishing something physically demanding like a triathlon, but without the fatigue.

Now, I'm not going to overstate the evidence here. Some of the more extreme claims about cold exposure are ahead of what the research currently supports. But there was a randomized controlled trial, the so-called Dutch shower study, which found that people who ended their showers with ninety seconds of cold water reported a twenty-nine percent reduction in self-reported sick days and significantly lower levels of fatigue compared to the control group. That's not bad for something that costs absolutely nothing and takes ninety seconds.

Movement number three is the Metabolic Walk, and this is perhaps the highest-leverage movement you can do for your long-term health. This happens immediately after meals, particularly after breakfast and lunch. I take Mango out again, or I just walk around the garden, but the key is that I move for fifteen to twenty minutes at a brisk pace after eating.

Here's the physiology. When you eat, glucose enters your bloodstream. If you remain sedentary after eating, your pancreas has to produce a large insulin response to deal with that glucose spike. However, when you walk soon after eating, even at a moderate pace, your muscles start drawing on that glucose directly for fuel. Instead of your pancreas working overtime, your muscles take the glucose up directly. This means your post-meal blood sugar curve is much flatter.

Over weeks and months of doing this, you are reducing the chronic burden on your insulin system, which has profound implications for your metabolic health, your weight, your inflammation levels, and your long-term disease risk. Insulin sensitivity tends to decline as we age, so this becomes increasingly important in your forties, fifties, and beyond.

A large prospective study in JAMA Internal Medicine following over eighty thousand adults found that people who walked briskly for at least seventy-five minutes per week had a twenty-four percent lower risk of premature death. Let me say that again. Just seventy-five minutes of brisk walking per week, which is barely ten minutes a day, was associated with a twenty-four percent lower risk of premature death. And those walking more saw benefits increase further, with no upper threshold where the protective effect stopped, though it does start to plateau.

For me, this happens after breakfast. We live somewhere hilly, so this is more of a workout than it might sound. Some genuinely steep climbs where my quads and hamstrings are working hard. I walk at a pace where I'm slightly out of breath but can still hold a conversation with Steph. We call this Zone Two aerobic training. It's the intensity where your mitochondria are working optimally to burn fat and glucose. And doing this fasted or after a meal makes a significant difference to your metabolic flexibility.

Movement number four is Resistance Work, and this is non-negotiable for longevity. From around age thirty-five, we naturally lose muscle mass through a process called sarcopenia. If you're not actively working against this, the downstream consequences are severe. Muscle loss is associated with falls, fractures, metabolic disease, insulin resistance, and progressive loss of physical function.

Grip strength, which is essentially a proxy for overall muscle strength, is one of the single strongest predictors of both lifespan and healthspan in the literature. A major study in The Lancet tracking nearly one hundred and forty thousand adults across seventeen countries found that every five-kilogram reduction in grip strength was associated with a seventeen percent increased risk of cardiovascular death and a sixteen percent higher risk of all-cause mortality.

Now, I do a dedicated weight session three or four times a week, usually around forty-five minutes alternating between push days, pull days, and core work. But the daily movement I want to focus on here is the maintenance of muscle through frequent, low-level resistance. This might be ten minutes of bodyweight work in the morning. Squats, press-ups, lunges. Or it might be carrying heavy shopping bags from the car. Or gardening. The key is that you're stimulating your muscles to remain sensitive to insulin and to maintain their mass.

Resistance training also preserves bone density, supports hormonal health, and has clear associations with reduced cognitive decline as you age. Just maintaining your ability to stand up from the floor without using your hands is a powerful predictor of longevity. I make a point of sitting on the floor when I play with George, who is now six months old, and getting up without using my hands. It sounds trivial, but try it. If you can't do it, that tells you something important about your current strength and mobility.

Movement number five is Spinal Mobility, and this is one that most people ignore until it's too late. As an emergency medicine doctor, I see the consequences of poor spinal health all the time. Compression fractures in the elderly, chronic back pain leading to immobility, falls caused by an inability to look over the shoulder. Your spine is the highway for your nervous system, and maintaining its mobility is crucial.

Every morning, I spend about five minutes doing what I call spinal hygiene. This includes cat-cow stretches to flex and extend the spine, thoracic rotations to maintain the ability to twist, and gentle hip flexor stretches because tight hip flexors from sitting pull your pelvis out of alignment and stress your lower back.

The cerebrospinal fluid that bathes your brain and spinal cord relies on movement to circulate properly. When you move your spine through its full range of motion, you are essentially pumping this fluid, which removes metabolic waste from your central nervous system and delivers nutrients. This is particularly important in the morning after lying still for eight hours.

Posture matters for longevity in ways that aren't immediately obvious. Forward head posture, that hunched position we get from looking at phones and computers, restricts breathing capacity, affects balance, and is associated with increased fall risk in older adults. Maintaining the ability to stand tall, look up, and rotate your trunk fully is fundamental to maintaining your independence.

Movement number six is Balance Training, and this targets your cerebellum and proprioceptive system. Every day, I practice standing on one leg. I do this while brushing my teeth in the morning. I stand on my left leg for ninety seconds, then my right leg for ninety seconds. When that becomes easy, I close my eyes. When that becomes easy, I turn my head from side to side while keeping my eyes closed.

Why does this matter? Falls are one of the leading causes of injury and death in people over sixty-five. A fall that results in a hip fracture has a mortality rate that shocks most people. But beyond fall prevention, balance training stimulates your cerebellum, which is involved in coordination, timing, and even cognitive processing. Studies have shown that proprioceptive training, which is essentially knowing where your body is in space, is associated with reduced rates of dementia.

Your vestibular system, located in your inner ear, is intimately connected to your spatial awareness and cognitive function. When you challenge your balance, you are forcing your brain to create new neural pathways. This is neuroplasticity in action. And like everything else, if you don't use it, you lose it.

I often do this while holding George, which adds an unstable load and makes it even more challenging. Or I'll do it on a cushion to create an unstable surface. The point is that you're creating controlled instability that forces your nervous system to adapt and strengthen.

Movement number seven is Diaphragmatic Breathing, which I know sounds like it isn't a movement, but it absolutely is. Your diaphragm is a muscle, and how you breathe affects every system in your body. Most people breathe too shallowly, using their chest and neck muscles rather than their diaphragm. This keeps us in a low-grade sympathetic nervous system state, essentially mild chronic stress.

Every day, usually around mid-morning when I'm back from my walk and doing my brain work, I do five minutes of what I call coherent breathing. This is breathing in for five seconds and out for five seconds, focusing on expanding your belly rather than your chest. This stimulates the vagus nerve, which is the main parasympathetic nerve running from your brain to your gut. It shifts you into rest-and-digest mode, lowers your heart rate, reduces inflammation, and improves heart rate variability.

Remember, heart rate variability, or HRV, is one of the most valuable metrics we have for longevity. A high HRV means your nervous system is flexible and adaptable. People who consistently have higher HRV tend to have better cardiovascular health, better stress resilience, and they age more favorably. There was a study in the Journal of the American College of Cardiology that found people in the lowest quartile of HRV had a risk of cardiovascular death more than double that of those in the highest quartile.

Controlled breathing is one of the most reliable ways to improve your HRV. It costs nothing. You can do it anywhere. And it serves as a movement break from whatever sedentary work you're doing. I check my HRV every morning on my Garmin, and I've noticed a clear correlation between days when I prioritize this breathing practice and higher HRV scores.

Movement number eight is Social Movement, and this is perhaps my favorite because it combines two of the most powerful longevity interventions into one. Walking with other people. Not sitting and having coffee. Not watching television together. But moving together.

The evidence on social connection and longevity is compelling. There was a huge meta-analysis drawing on data from over three hundred thousand people that found strong social relationships were associated with a fifty percent increased likelihood of survival. That puts social connection on par with quitting smoking and far ahead of most medical interventions.

When you walk and talk with someone, you're getting the metabolic benefits of movement plus the psychological benefits of connection. You're often walking slower than you would alone, which keeps you in that beneficial Zone Two heart rate. You're distracted by the conversation, so the time passes more easily. And you're holding yourself accountable to show up because someone else is waiting for you.

I do this with Steph, obviously, and our walks with Mango often turn into social time where we discuss the day. But I also make a point of walking with friends or calling family while I'm on my post-meal walks. Even a voice note while walking is better than a text while sitting. The movement matters, and the connection matters, and doing them together is synergistic.

Now, I know what many of you are thinking at this point. This sounds like a lot. When exactly am I supposed to fit all of this in? So let me pull this together and tell you exactly how my day actually looks with these eight movements fitted in.

I wake up at six a.m., usually naturally about ten minutes before the alarm because I've kept my sleep schedule consistent. The first thing I do is check my HRV and sleep score on my Garmin, which takes thirty seconds. Then immediately, before anything else, I take Mango out for the Circadian Walk. This is five to ten minutes outside, getting that light exposure, regardless of weather. We're back by quarter past six.

Then I shower, ending with the Thermal Transition, the cold blast for two to three minutes. By six thirty, I'm hydrated and having breakfast with Steph and George. Immediately after eating, around seven a.m., I take Mango out again for the Metabolic Walk. This is fifteen to twenty minutes, brisk pace, up the hills. This is non-negotiable.

Back by seven thirty, I do five minutes of Spinal Mobility and Balance Training while I brush my teeth and get ready. Standing on one leg, cat-cow stretches, hip flexor opens. Then from eight until nine thirty is my brain work time, but I start this with five minutes of Diaphragmatic Breathing to get my nervous system into the right state.

At some point during the morning, usually around ten or eleven, I do my dedicated Resistance Work session, but remember, the daily maintenance of muscle happens throughout the day. Squats while waiting for the kettle to boil. Carrying George up the stairs. These count.

The Social Movement happens throughout. The morning walk with Steph. A phone call with a friend during the afternoon post-lunch walk. And if I'm not working an evening shift in the A&E, an after-dinner walk with the family.

When you write it out like this, it looks quite involved, but in practice, it flows naturally. The Circadian Walk becomes automatic. The cold shower becomes a habit you look forward to. The post-meal walks are simply how you digest. And the breathing and balance work take minutes.

The compounding effect of doing these eight movements consistently, day after day, week after week, is where the real benefit comes. You're maintaining your metabolic health, your muscle mass, your balance, your nervous system regulation, your circadian rhythm, and your social connections. These are the actual drivers of longevity.

Now, obviously, I understand that not everybody watching this can replicate this exact schedule. Some of you are working night shifts, some have young children that wake at different times, some have mobility limitations. But the principles remain. Get light in your eyes early. Move after you eat. Maintain your strength and balance. Breathe deliberately. Connect with others while moving.

You don't need supplements. You don't need expensive equipment. You don't need a gym membership, though they're useful. You need consistency with these fundamental movements that humans have been doing for millennia until very recently when we decided that sitting still all day was normal.

The common theme running through all of these videos on this channel is that small things done consistently and improved over time is where the real health gains come from. These eight movements are not exercises in the sense of punishment or extreme effort. They are restoration of normal human function. They are the baseline of what your body expects from you to remain healthy into your eighties, nineties, and beyond.

If you found this useful, then please do subscribe. I've got videos coming up on specific strength protocols, on the science of sleep timing, and on metabolic health markers you can track at home. Thank you for watching, and I'll see you in the next one.
topic: Do These 8 Movements Every Day to Live Longer
title: Do These 8 Movements Every Day to Live Longer
